sourceMovie.title: Ikiru
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Ikiru (1952) stuck because it commits to its drama premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Akira Kurosawa's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: The Prestige
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Ikiru clicked for you, The Prestige (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Lost in Translation
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Ikiru clicked for you, Lost in Translation (2003) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Bridge to Terabithia
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Ikiru clicked for you, Bridge to Terabithia (2007) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Ikiru?
faq.answer: Try The Prestige, Lost in Translation, Bridge to Terabithia—each shares DNA with Ikiru while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Ikiru?
faq.answer: Ikiru (1952) is best described as Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Ikiru worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
